title: Beijing Tells Top AI Startups to Refuse US Money Without Approval
subtitle: The new guidance — extending well beyond the Manus probe — targets ByteDance, Moonshot, and StepFun as China hardens controls on cross-border AI capital.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Chinese regulators have told several of the country's most prominent AI startups to reject US capital in funding rounds without explicit government approval, according to a Bloomberg report on April 24. The National Development and Reform Commission and other agencies have given the guidance to Moonshot AI, StepFun, and ByteDance — the owner of TikTok and one of China's most popular AI chatbots — among others. The restriction also covers secondary share sales, which are how many late-stage Chinese tech employees and early investors typically cash out.

The trigger was Meta's $2 billion acquisition of Manus, the Chinese AI agent startup, announced late last year. That deal prompted a Beijing probe into illegal foreign investment and tech exports, and the new guidance generalizes from one transaction into a sector-wide policy: Chinese AI is now treated as strategically sensitive, in roughly the way semiconductors and rare earths already are. US investors who hold stakes in firms like Moonshot or StepFun may find their positions hard to scale up, hard to exit, or both.

The move closes one of the last open seams in the US–China tech relationship. Washington has spent three years restricting US chip exports to China and US investment in Chinese AI under outbound investment rules. Beijing is now pulling the corresponding lever from its side. The combined effect is two AI ecosystems that share fewer investors, fewer chips, fewer engineers, and fewer model weights than at any point since 2023. Chinese labs — DeepSeek, Moonshot, ByteDance's Doubao — will continue to compete at the frontier, but the capital and supply chain backing them will look increasingly domestic.

For learners, this is a reminder that AI is now governed as much by capital controls and export licenses as by research papers. If you are following the field, watching where the money can and cannot flow tells you almost as much as watching benchmarks. A model that is technically open-weight but built by a company barred from accepting foreign investment is a different artifact than one built by a global consortium — even if the architecture diagrams look identical.

sources.title: China to Curb US Investment in Tech Companies After Meta Deal — Bloomberg
